2.47.sankara: You have a right to perform your prescribed duty, but you are not entitled to the fruits of action. Never consider yourself to be the cause of the results of your activities, and never be attached to not doing your duty.
2.47.meaning: This verse contains the essence of Karma Yoga - the path of selfless action. Krishna teaches Arjuna that one should perform their duties without attachment to the results. The focus should be on the action itself, not on what one will gain from it. This detachment from fruits leads to mental peace and spiritual growth.
2.47.application: In modern life, this teaching applies to our work and relationships. When we work without anxiety about outcomes, we perform better and remain mentally balanced. Whether in career, studies, or personal goals, focusing on sincere effort rather than results reduces stress and increases fulfillment.

2.20.sankara: For the soul, there is neither birth nor death at any time. He has not come into being, does not come into being, and will not come into being. He is unborn, eternal, ever-existing and primeval. He is not slain when the body is slain.
2.20.meaning: This verse establishes the fundamental truth of the immortality of the soul (Atman). Krishna explains that the true self is beyond birth and death. It is eternal consciousness that merely changes bodies like we change clothes. This understanding helps overcome the fear of death.
2.20.application: This teaching brings comfort during times of loss and helps overcome the fear of mortality. Understanding that we are spiritual beings having a human experience transforms how we approach life and death.

3.19.sankara: Without attachment, perform the work that has to be done, for working without attachment, a man attains the Supreme. By performing work without attachment, one reaches the Supreme.
3.19.meaning: This verse emphasizes that action is inevitable - even to maintain the body, one must act. The key is to perform all actions as a duty, without desire for rewards. Such selfless action purifies the mind and leads to liberation.
3.19.application: Everyday tasks become spiritual practice when done with the right attitude. Whether cooking, cleaning, or working, performing duties with dedication but without ego or expectation transforms mundane activities into yoga.

4.7.sankara: Whenever and wherever there is a decline in religious practice, O descendant of Bharata, and a predominant rise of irreligion—at that time I descend Myself.
4.7.meaning: Here Krishna explains the purpose of divine incarnations (Avataras). When righteousness declines and unrighteousness rises, the divine manifests to restore dharma. This happens not just in ancient times but whenever needed.
4.7.application: This verse reminds us that divine help is always available when we sincerely seek it. It also inspires us to be agents of positive change in the world, helping restore harmony wherever we find disorder.

4.8.sankara: To deliver the pious and to annihilate the miscreants, as well as to reestablish the principles of religion, I Myself appear, millennium after millennium.
4.8.meaning: Krishna elaborates on the mission of divine incarnations: protection of the good, destruction of evil, and reestablishment of dharma. This happens periodically to maintain cosmic order.
4.8.application: This teaching reassures that good ultimately prevails over evil. It encourages us to stand for righteousness, knowing that the divine supports those who uphold truth and justice.

5.6.sankara: That person who neither rejoices upon achieving something pleasant nor laments upon obtaining something unpleasant, who is self-intelligent, who is unbewildered, and who knows the science of God, is already situated in transcendence.
5.6.meaning: True yoga is maintaining equilibrium in all circumstances - neither excessively happy in gain nor excessively sad in loss. Such a person of steady wisdom is considered perfected in yoga.
5.6.application: This equanimity is the foundation of mental health. Modern psychology confirms that emotional regulation, not suppression, leads to wellbeing. Practicing balanced responses to life's ups and downs builds resilience.

6.5.sankara: One must elevate—not degrade—oneself by one's own mind. The mind is the friend of the conditioned soul, and his enemy as well.
6.5.meaning: The mind can be our greatest ally or worst enemy depending on how we use it. Self-mastery comes from controlling and directing the mind positively rather than letting it control us.
6.5.application: Mindfulness and meditation practices help master the mind. When we observe thoughts without attachment, we can choose which to nurture and which to release, becoming masters rather than victims of our mental states.

6.6.sankara: For him who has conquered the mind, the mind is the best of friends; but for one who has failed to do so, his mind will remain the greatest enemy.
6.6.meaning: This verse continues the teaching on mind mastery. A controlled mind leads to peace and success, while an uncontrolled mind creates inner chaos and external problems.
6.6.application: Daily meditation, even briefly, strengthens mind control. Like training a muscle, consistent practice makes the mind our servant rather than master, leading to clarity and peace.

9.22.sankara: But those who always worship Me with exclusive devotion, meditating on My transcendental form—to them I carry what they lack, and I preserve what they have.
9.22.meaning: Krishna promises His personal care for those who dedicate themselves to Him with devotion. The Lord personally takes responsibility for the welfare of His devotees.
9.22.application: This verse brings deep comfort and trust in divine protection. It encourages surrendering worries to the divine while continuing to do our part, knowing we are cared for.

10.20.sankara: I am the Self, O Gudakesa, seated in the hearts of all creatures. I am the beginning, the middle and the end of all beings.
10.20.meaning: Krishna reveals that He is the innermost Self (Atman) dwelling in all beings. He is the source, sustenance, and ultimate destination of everything that exists.
10.20.application: Seeing the divine in everyone transforms relationships and reduces conflict. Recognizing the same consciousness in all beings fosters compassion, equality, and unity.

12.13.sankara: One who is not envious but is a kind friend to all living entities, who does not think himself a proprietor and is free from false ego, who is equal in both happiness and distress, who is tolerant, always satisfied, self-controlled...
12.13.meaning: This verse describes the qualities of a dear devotee: non-enviousness, kindness, freedom from false ego, equanimity, tolerance, contentment, and self-control. These qualities make one dear to God.
12.13.application: These qualities form the foundation of spiritual character. Practicing kindness, humility, and contentment in daily life makes one a true devotee regardless of external rituals.

13.14.sankara: Everywhere are His hands, legs, eyes, faces, and ears. He exists as the all-pervading Supersoul encompassing everything.
13.14.meaning: This verse describes the omnipresence of the Divine. The Supreme Being pervades everything and exists simultaneously everywhere through His countless senses.
13.14.application: Understanding divine omnipresence transforms ordinary spaces into sacred ones. The divine is as present in our homes and workplaces as in temples, encouraging us to live with awareness and reverence.

15.15.sankara: I am seated in everyone's heart, and from Me come memory, knowledge and forgetfulness. By all the Vedas am I to be known; indeed I am the compiler of Vedanta, and I am the knower of the Vedas.
15.15.meaning: Krishna is the source of all knowledge and the ultimate purpose of the Vedas. As the Supersoul in all hearts, He grants and withdraws knowledge according to the seeker's sincerity.
15.15.application: This verse encourages sincere seeking of truth. It suggests that true understanding comes when the divine grace facilitates it. Humility in learning and respect for all knowledge sources is implied.

16.21.sankara: There are three gates leading to this hell—lust, anger and greed. Every sane man should give these up, for they lead to the degradation of the soul.
16.21.meaning: Three enemies of spiritual life are identified: kama (lust), krodha (anger), and lobha (greed). These destroy discrimination and lead to spiritual downfall.
16.21.application: Recognizing these three forces helps guard against them. When desires, anger, or greed arise, awareness allows us to pause and choose differently, protecting our peace and integrity.

18.66.sankara: Abandon all varieties of religion and just surrender unto Me. I shall deliver you from all sinful reactions. Do not fear.
18.66.meaning: The essence of the entire Gita is contained in this verse. Krishna asks for complete surrender (sharanagati) as the ultimate path. He promises protection and liberation to those who fully surrender.
18.66.application: Complete surrender means trusting divine wisdom over limited human understanding. It doesn't mean inaction but acting with faith that the divine order supports our highest good.
